retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Melanoma -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A whole-body skin examination is the most essential and fundamental evaluation needed in diagnosing melanoma, and it should be performed in the presence of optimal lighting. Skin examination is often complemented with a dermatoscopy of suspicious lesions. Using this evaluation, the assessment of "ABCDE" is completed. During this examination, attention must be paid to examining nail units. This is important for properly diagnosing abnormal melanomas, which follow a slightly different mnemonic ABCDEF, where the "F" stands for family or personal history of melanoma.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Melanoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The characteristic signs of early melanoma are recognized with the following well-known ABCDE mnemonic: “ A ” stands for A symmetry “ B ” stands for B order: irregular, ragged, notched, or blurred edges “ C ” stands for C olor: nonuniform or variegated “ D ” stands for D iameter: larger than 6 millimeters “ E ” stands for E volving : changes in size, shape, or color [25]

retrieved_docs.title: Metastatic Melanoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: The characteristic signs of early melanoma are recognized with the well-known ABCDE mnemonic as follows: “ A ” stands for A symmetry “ B ” stands for B order: irregular, ragged, notched, or blurred edges “ C ” stands for C olor: nonuniform or variegated “ D ” stands for D iameter: larger than 6 millimeters “ E ” stands for E volving : changes in size, shape, or color [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Melanoma of the Head and Neck -- History and Physical -- General Melanoma Physical Examination Information
retrieved_docs.content: When considering melanoma in physical examination, patients may have a new lesion or existing mole with changing characteristics. However, amelanotic melanoma will not have typical dark pigment from melanin. Amelanotic melanoma may be pink, red, purple, or normal skin color. The characteristics of melanoma are commonly known by the acronym ABCDE and include the following: A: Asymmetry B: Irregular border C: Color variations, especially red, white, and blue tones in a brown or black lesion D: Diameter greater than 6 mm

retrieved_docs.title: Dermatoscopic Characteristics of Melanoma Versus Benign Lesions and Nonmelanoma Cancers -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: While a naked eye examination can be useful, it has been observed that up to 40% of melanomas may be misclassified when clinicians rely solely on the ABCDE rule. [4] The ABCDE rule includes asymmetry, border irregularity, color variegation, diameter greater than 6 mm, and evolution. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Atypical Multiple Mole and Melanoma Syndrome -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: A thorough dermatologic evaluation is essential for identifying suspicious lesions and assessing disease burden in individuals with FAMMM syndrome. Initial evaluation should also include a thorough personal/family history, including screening for melanoma and pancreatic cancer among first, second, and third-degree relatives. [3] Dermoscopy may aid in the identification of suspicious features associated with melanoma. Atypical nevi should be evaluated via the ABCDE "Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolution) characteristics. Total body photography and sequential digital dermoscopy imaging may also be utilized for longitudinal monitoring of nevi and early detection of changes suggestive of malignant transformation.

retrieved_docs.title: Acral Lentiginous Melanoma -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: It has been proposed the that classical “ABCDE” approach for melanoma be replaced on acral surfaces with the “CUBED” acronym which stands for colored lesions where any part is a different color, uncertain diagnosis or a lesion without a clear clinical diagnosis, bleeding lesions, enlargement of a lesion, and a delay in healing beyond two months. [27]

retrieved_docs.title: Familial Atypical Multiple Mole and Melanoma Syndrome -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Patients should also be instructed on how to perform regular skin self-examinations, focusing on changes in the size, shape, color, or texture of existing nevi and the appearance of new lesions. Clinicians should provide guidance on the "ABCDE" criteria (Asymmetry, Border, Color, Diameter, Evolution) and encourage prompt medical evaluation of any suspicious findings.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Melanoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify the pathophysiology of malignant melanoma. Interpret diagnostic studies recommended for malignant melanoma. Implement treatment strategies for various stages of nonmetastatic malignant melanoma. Identify the role of the interprofessional team in managing a patient with malignant melanoma. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Malignant Melanoma: Guideline-Based Management and Interprofessional Collaboration -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: By embracing interprofessional communication and care coordination, healthcare teams can reduce delays, minimize errors, and support shared decision-making that respects patient values. This model of care aligns with the principles of interprofessional continuing education (IPCE) and reflects the growing recognition that high-quality cancer care depends on the seamless collaboration of all healthcare team members.

retrieved_docs.title: Lentigo Maligna Melanoma -- Deterrence and Patient Education
retrieved_docs.content: Due to the causative nature of chronic UVR damage in inducing lentigo maligna/LMM, diligent sun protection is key to prevention. The American Academy of Dermatology (AAD) periodically publishes guidelines on preventing and treating skin cancer. [61] [77] Patients should wear broad-spectrum sunscreen (SPF ≥ 30) whenever outdoors, reapply every 2 hours, and immediately after swimming. They should use a sufficient amount (approximately 1 ounce or equivalent to 1 shot glass) to cover the entire body. Wearing ultraviolet protection factor clothing, avoiding the sun between peak hours of 10 am to 3 pm, and seeking shade provides additional sun protection. Furthermore, patients should be aware of the ABCDE criteria for melanoma (asymmetry, border, color, diameter, and evolving), perform monthly skin self-examinations, and seek professional care by a dermatologist or primary care clinician for any concerning lesions (see Image. Melanoma Detection).

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Malignant Melanoma: Guideline-Based Management and Interprofessional Collaboration -- Function -- Diagnostic Assessment for Cutaneous Melanoma
retrieved_docs.content: Cutaneous pigmented lesions characterized by dynamic changes in the shape, color, diameter, and borders (ABCD rule) are suspicious for melanoma [9] . Dermoscopy can be used as a diagnostic aid to improve the accuracy of tissue sampling and is endorsed by all the clinical practice guidelines. Full-thickness biopsy is recommended for diagnosis; AAD and NCCN recommend a negative margin of around 1-3 mm, while CCA recommends a margin of 2 mm and upper subcutis. ESMO does not specify the margin distance while recommending minimal margins with the full-thickness biopsy. A partial-thickness biopsy is acceptable for the face or acral surfaces.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Melanoma -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity for healthcare professionals is designed to enhance the learner's competence when managing malignant melanoma, equipping them with updated knowledge, skills, and strategies for timely identification, effective interventions, prevention, and improved care coordination, leading to better patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Melanoma Metastatic to the Central Nervous System -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Review the etiology of malignant melanoma metastatic to the central nervous system. Describe the evaluation of patients with malignant melanoma metastatic to the central nervous system. Review the management considerations for patients with malignant melanoma metastatic to the central nervous system. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Cutaneous Malignant Melanoma: Guideline-Based Management and Interprofessional Collaboration -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Interpret epidemiological data to understand current trends in the incidence and prevalence of cutaneous melanoma in the United States. Compare NCCN, Cancer Council Australia, AAD, and ESMO guideline recommendations for diagnosing cutaneous malignant melanoma. Identify guideline-based recommendations for genetic counseling evaluation in patients with a personal or family history of cutaneous malignant melanoma. Coordinate post-treatment surveillance strategies as an interprofessional team to ensure comprehensive long-term management of patients with cutaneous malignant melanoma. Access free multiple choice questions on this topic.

retrieved_docs.title: Atypical Mole -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Atypical moles, or dysplastic nevi, are acquired melanocytic neoplasms with disordered architecture and atypia of melanocytes. Multiple synonyms for atypical moles; they are also known as Clark nevi, B-K moles, or dysplastic nevi, although strictly speaking, the term dysplastic nevus refers only to a specific microscopic appearance, of which, only a minority of atypical moles fulfill the criteria. Clinically, dysplastic nevi resemble melanoma since they often share ABCDE morphological melanoma features.

Collectively, these interrelated entities form a hierarchical and functional network that connects genetic syndromes, primary gastrointestinal cancers, various metastatic malignancies, and skin melanomas, providing essential context for accurate diagnosis, staging, and management in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Melanomas of skin are malignant neoplasms that arise from melanocytes in the cutaneous tissue and represent a specific form of skin cancer. سرطان الشفة الخبيث, which translates to malignant lip cancer, is a distinct malignant tumor that originates in the lip and is classified as an oral cavity cancer. Although both conditions are cancers, they affect different anatomical sites and are not subtypes of one another; each requires separate diagnostic and therapeutic approaches tailored to its location and biological behavior. Understanding the differences between melanomas of skin and سرطان الشفة الخبيث is important for accurate diagnosis, appropriate treatment planning, and effective patient care.

Together, these hierarchical, mapping, and membership relationships provide clinicians and researchers with an integrated taxonomy for organ‑specific cancers, metastatic pathways, and non‑hematologic malignant disease classification, facilitating diagnosis, research, and therapeutic decision‑making.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2356
retrieved_docs.content: Other non‑epithelial cancer of skin is a broader classification that encompasses the condition described in Arabic as ورم خبيث في الجلد، آخر, indicating an additional malignant tumor of the skin.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2178
retrieved_docs.content: The medical concept “سرطان الجلد الخبيث” (malignant skin cancer) is mapped from the broader description “ورم ثانوي خبيث في مواضع محددة أخرى” (malignant secondary tumor in other specific locations), indicating that malignant skin cancer is understood as a form of malignant secondary tumor that can arise in particular anatomical sites. This relationship clarifies that the skin manifestation of a malignant secondary tumor is classified under the specific term “سرطان الجلد الخبيث,” linking the general category of secondary malignant neoplasms to its specific cutaneous presentation.

retrieved_docs.content: The skin disorder حُمامَى is linked to other inflammatory conditions of the skin, indicating that it shares pathogenic mechanisms and clinical associations with additional inflammatory dermatoses. This “associated_with” relationship highlights the relevance of recognizing حُمامَى within the broader spectrum of skin inflammation for accurate diagnosis and comprehensive patient management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 4 -- Community 2848
retrieved_docs.content: حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ and ربط البوق are medical concepts that frequently co‑occur, indicating that the presence of حَمْلٌ بُوقِيّ is often associated with the finding or procedure ربط البوق. This consistent association suggests that clinicians should be aware of the link between these two entities, as recognizing one may prompt evaluation for the other, thereby informing diagnosis and guiding appropriate patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: الحرشفية في الرأس والعنق) and its metastatic counterpart illustrate the inverse‑isa relationship between primary and secondary disease. The SMQ also encompasses pancreatic cancer and melanomas of the skin. Malignant skin cancer (سرطان الجلد الخبيث) serves as the parent concept for melanomas of the skin, which may involve the retina (ret) as a documented finding site, indicating possible ocular involvement. Melanomas of the skin arise from cutaneous melanocytes, whereas malignant lip cancer originates from epithelial cells of the lip; despite an inverse‑isa link in some representations, they are correctly classified as separate aggressive malignancies requiring distinct diagnostic and therapeutic approaches. Neoplasms of uncertain behavior are linked to metastasis, emphasizing the clinical importance of assessing metastatic potential even when the primary lesion’s benign versus malignant nature is unclear. In the hierarchical structure of clinical actions, Excision serves as a parent concept for Clinical actions A‑G and is associated with an answer to a colorectal cancer diagnostic procedure query. Overall, Excision functions as an umbrella term that groups diverse operative interventions while delineating specific techniques that are not considered part of its scope, thereby aiding clinicians in categorizing and selecting appropriate surgical strategies.

In the context of سرطانة العظم, Tumor cells, malignant are identified as the abnormal cellular component that drives the malignancy. Together, the presence of malignant tumor cells and the detection of metastatic lesions provide critical information for diagnosing, staging, and managing سرطانة العظم, guiding therapeutic decisions and prognostic assessment.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 2719
retrieved_docs.content: The concept “kanker; metastase” (cancer metastasis) is linked by the “has child” relationship to the specific condition “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” (metastatic skin cancer), indicating that metastatic skin cancer is a child entity within the broader category of cancer metastasis. This relationship clarifies that “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” is a subtype of “kanker; metastase,” situating it within the overall framework of metastatic malignancies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2616
retrieved_docs.content: The term “kanker; metastase” denotes the broader concept of cancer with metastatic spread and includes as specific child entities the condition “سرطان الجلد النقيلي” (metastatic skin cancer) and the process “انتشار إلى العظم” (bone metastasis), indicating that cancer can manifest by spreading from the skin to other sites and by extending into bone tissue.
